name: Tulsa Race Massacre
description: The Tulsa Race Massacre, also known as the Tulsa Race Riot, was a violent attack on the Greenwood District in Tulsa, Oklahoma, in 1921. Greenwood, a prosperous Black community nicknamed "Black Wall Street," was systematically destroyed by white mobs. The event began after an alleged assault of a white woman by a Black man, sparking racial tensions and ultimately leading to widespread violence. Proponents argue that the massacre was not a spontaneous riot but a pre-planned act of racial terror, citing the coordinated nature of the attacks, the use of airplanes to drop incendiary devices, and the destruction of property. They claim that official records were suppressed and the event was deliberately erased from historical narratives to minimize the culpability of white residents and institutions. Mainstream historical perspective acknowledges the event as a tragic and devastating act of racial violence. However, debates exist regarding the extent of pre-planning versus spontaneous escalation, and the motivations of participants. Scholarly research has focused on uncovering the full extent of the destruction, identifying victims, and examining the long-term social and economic consequences for the Black community in Tulsa. While the event was long overlooked in mainstream history, recent efforts have aimed to provide a more accurate and comprehensive account of the massacre and its legacy, acknowledging the systemic racism that enabled such an event to occur and the ongoing struggle for racial justice.
subtitle: Racial Violence and the Destruction of Greenwood

claims.claim: The massacre was a pre-planned act of racial terrorism.
claims.evidence: Coordinated attacks, use of airplanes with incendiary devices, testimonies from survivors describing organized efforts to destroy Greenwood.
claims.criticism: While evidence suggests some level of organization, it's debated whether it was a fully premeditated plan or a rapid escalation of existing racial tensions. Some actions could be attributed to mob mentality.

claims.claim: Official records were deliberately suppressed to cover up the extent of white culpability.
claims.evidence: Missing documents, altered reports, and a general lack of acknowledgement of the event in official historical narratives for many years.
claims.criticism: While some records were undoubtedly lost or suppressed, the extent of a deliberate cover-up is debated. Loss of records may also be attributed to the chaos of the event and subsequent neglect. More recently, efforts have been made to recover and analyze existing documents.

claims.claim: The economic impact of the massacre continues to affect the Black community in Tulsa today.
claims.evidence: Loss of generational wealth, displacement of residents, and the destruction of a thriving business district created long-term economic disadvantages.
claims.criticism: While the massacre undeniably had a devastating economic impact, attributing current economic disparities solely to this event is an oversimplification. Other factors such as systemic racism in housing, employment, and education also contribute to these disparities.

keyFigures.name: Buck Colbert Franklin
keyFigures.role: Lawyer who defended Black residents during and after the massacre.

keyFigures.name: Scott Ellsworth
keyFigures.role: Historian who has extensively researched and written about the Tulsa Race Massacre.

keyFigures.name: Alfred Brophy
keyFigures.role: Legal scholar who has analyzed the legal and historical aspects of the massacre.

mythologyConnections: The idea of a lost golden age (Greenwood) destroyed by malevolent forces.
The concept of a scapegoat (the accused Black man) used to justify violence.

timeline.event: Alleged assault of a white woman by a Black man, Dick Rowland.
Tulsa Race Massacre: White mobs attack and destroy Greenwood.
Efforts to rebuild Greenwood and seek justice for the victims.
Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921 releases its report.
Centennial commemoration of the Tulsa Race Massacre.
